top_comments.text:
I can pay for parking here in Edinburgh using the RingGo app - which is incredibly convenient, means I don't have to hunt for change - which I rarely have as I mostly pay for everything else with a card.

I love the way it reminds you if your paid for parking period expires and gives you the option of extending by paying more - all without having to return to my car.

Edit: I also don't pay for parking very often - which means I'm even less likely to have change on me or

One reason why Air Asia is cheaper in MYR is because it's a Malaysian company, so they don't take foreign exchange risk nor pay bank fees when selling to you in ringgit.

For even more fun, try buying KTM (Malaysian rail) tickets in MYR vs. e.g. SGD. They will charge you the same numeric price, ignoring the exchange rate of about 2.5:1. This works much the same as the airlines pricing by sales region.

You'd want to be careful that you're viewing the same price package when doing this. For example, when displaying a ticket price in Australia, it must be an all-inclusive price - including all airport fees, surcharges, and everything else. Viewing the same ticket from, say, Japan, it might appear cheaper but it doesn't have those same inclusions, and when you add them all back in it comes out pretty similar.

That said, I always buy my air asia flights in malaysian ringgits - it

npm_packages.name: @desivocal/agents-cdn
npm_packages.description: Free Text To speech and AI Voice generator

contrarian_analysis.bull_case_flaw: Ringg touts latency, language support, and call volume, but sidesteps the hardest part: truly human-like, context-aware conversations at scale. There's no evidence their AI is meaningfully better than the generic LLM-powered voice bots flooding the market, and they offer no proof of superior outcomes or unique IP.
contrarian_analysis.elephant_in_room: How do they prevent their AI agents from getting stuck, misunderstood, or causing brand damage in complex real-world calls—especially compared to incumbents with years of telephony and NLP experience?

timing_assessment.reasoning: Programmable voice AI for enterprise is a crowded, commoditized space with Twilio, Google, Microsoft, and a dozen VC-backed startups already offering similar APIs and no-code call center automation. The window for a new platform to become a category leader is closing fast unless they have a step-function improvement, which isn't evident here.

incumbent_threat.most_dangerous_competitor: OpenAI (with Microsoft distribution) or Google Cloud
incumbent_threat.killer_feature: A native GPT-5-powered voice agent API with direct integration into Teams/Workspace, one-click CRM/telephony setup, and usage-based pricing bundled with existing cloud credits. If OpenAI or Google launches this, Ringg becomes a feature—not a company.
incumbent_threat.time_to_threat: 3-6 months (these incumbents already have the pieces, just not packaged for this vertical yet)

hidden_assumptions: Enterprises will trust a startup with sensitive customer voice data and compliance requirements.
Ringg can maintain cost-effective, reliable voice AI infrastructure at scale without the deep pockets or global infrastructure of hyperscalers.
Their AI can handle real-world conversational edge cases as well as (or better than) incumbents.
Integration with CRMs and business systems will be seamless and robust enough for enterprise needs.
LLM and voice synthesis costs will continue to fall, allowing sustainable margins.

moat_reality.claimed_moat: Enterprise-grade performance (low latency, high concurrency), 20+ languages, easy API/SDK integration, and vertical-specific solutions.
moat_reality.actual_moat: Minimal. All these features are rapidly becoming table stakes as LLM and telephony APIs commoditize. No evidence of proprietary models, data network effects, or irreplicable integrations.

contrarian_analysis.honest_take: Ringg is an API wrapper around commodity LLM and telephony tech with nice dashboards, but nothing defensible. If OpenAI, Google, or Twilio decide to care, Ringg gets wiped out overnight.

primary_trigger.trigger: Recent advances in AI analytics platforms tailored for manufacturing data streams
primary_trigger.evidence: Breakthroughs in real-time data processing and machine learning models optimized for industrial IoT have reached production-grade reliability in 2023-2024

secondary_triggers.trigger: Manufacturing companies are dramatically increasing digital transformation budgets post-pandemic
secondary_triggers.relevance: Ringg can capitalize on urgent demand for AI-driven efficiency and predictive maintenance solutions

secondary_triggers.trigger: Global supply chain regulations now require granular traceability and reporting
secondary_triggers.relevance: Ringg’s analytics can help manufacturers comply with stricter standards and avoid penalties

secondary_triggers.trigger: Competitors focus on generic AI solutions with insufficient customization for manufacturing
secondary_triggers.relevance: Ringg can win by delivering industry-specific features and integrations

secondary_triggers.trigger: Manufacturing teams have adopted remote monitoring and digital dashboards as a norm
secondary_triggers.relevance: Ringg’s software fits seamlessly into new workflows and expectations

why_now.why_not_earlier: Two years ago, AI models and data infrastructure were not mature enough to handle the scale and specificity required for manufacturing environments.
why_now.why_not_later: Delaying would allow competitors to capture key partnerships and data integrations, making it harder for Ringg to become the industry standard.
why_now.newsletter_hook: Manufacturing’s AI moment is now—Ringg rides the wave of new tech, urgent budgets, and regulatory pressure.

takeaways.title: Decouple Knowledge Base from Call Flow Logic
takeaways.insight: Ringg separates the knowledge base (FAQs, SOPs, docs) from the voice agent's call flow logic, allowing for rapid updates to information without redeploying or editing flow logic. This modularity reduces operational friction and speeds up iteration.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Design your conversational AI or workflow systems so that the business logic (flows, state machines) is separate from the underlying knowledge/content. Use a retrieval layer (e.g., RAG or vector search) to fetch up-to-date answers at runtime.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your use case requires tightly coupled, deterministic flows (e.g., compliance-critical IVRs) where knowledge updates must be reviewed with logic changes.

takeaways.title: Optimize for Ultra-Low Latency in Real-Time Voice AI
takeaways.insight: Ringg achieves sub-337ms mean latency by tightly integrating real-time inference with telephony (WebRTC/SIP) and possibly custom infrastructure. Prioritizing the entire path from user to model inference to response is essential for natural, human-like conversations.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Profile your voice AI stack end-to-end. Minimize hops between services, colocate inference near telephony gateways, and use streaming APIs for both ASR and TTS. Consider custom or optimized LLM serving layers.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your application is text-only or not latency sensitive (e.g., async support bots).

takeaways.title: No-Code Deployment for Custom Voice Agents
takeaways.insight: Ringg provides a no-code interface for building and deploying voice assistants, lowering the barrier for non-engineers and speeding up iteration. This approach empowers business teams to manage AI agents directly.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Expose a visual builder or declarative config for your AI workflows. Allow uploads of knowledge sources and provide instant preview/deploy. Use schema validation to prevent invalid configs.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your users are exclusively engineers who prefer code-first APIs, or if your flows are too complex for visual tools.

takeaways.title: Vertical Data Moats via Domain-Specific Knowledge Uploads
takeaways.insight: Ringg lets users upload large (up to 25MB) knowledge bases, creating vertical data moats. This allows for highly specialized, context-rich agents that competitors can't easily replicate.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Support bulk upload and incremental update of domain-specific documents. Use RAG or knowledge graph techniques to index and retrieve relevant information at inference time.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your product is general-purpose and doesn't benefit from deep domain adaptation.

takeaways.title: Selective LLM Invocation via Knowledge Graphs
takeaways.insight: By using knowledge graphs or retrieval layers to answer common queries, Ringg likely reduces unnecessary LLM calls, saving on compute and latency. Only escalate to LLMs for ambiguous or novel questions.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Implement a retrieval-first pipeline: attempt to answer from indexed knowledge or FAQ; only invoke LLMs when confidence is low or no match is found.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If all queries are highly novel or require generative reasoning.

quick_wins: Profile your voice AI stack for end-to-end latency and identify slowest hops.
Add support for knowledge base uploads and decouple from flow logic.
Implement retrieval-first pipelines to reduce LLM invocations.
deeper_dives: Explore custom real-time inference serving for ultra-low latency voice AI.
Research RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) and knowledge graph integration.
Investigate no-code/low-code platforms for AI workflow deployment.

headlines.headline: Why Ringg is betting AGAINST the 'LLM as commodity' narrative in voice AI
headlines.subheadline: Ringg claims sub-337ms latency at 10,000+ concurrent calls—are custom stacks the real moat in a world of generic APIs?

headlines.headline: I reverse-engineered Ringg's real-time stack—here's how they handle 20+ accents at sub-337ms latency
headlines.subheadline: A deep dive into Ringg's architecture reveals surprising optimizations that most voice AI APIs skip.

headlines.headline: Twilio for manufacturing, but with sub-337ms AI calls: Ringg's vertical playbook
headlines.subheadline: Ringg is fusing agentic architectures and vertical data moats—will this niche focus outlast the API giants?

headlines.headline: The latency obsession every AI infra team should steal from Ringg
headlines.subheadline: Ringg's sub-337ms mean latency isn’t just marketing—it's a masterclass in real-time inference at scale.

headlines.headline: Is Ringg just an API wrapper with dashboards—or a real infra breakthrough? Let's find out.
headlines.subheadline: With OpenAI, Google, and Twilio lurking, does Ringg have anything truly defensible under the hood?

best_headline.headline: I reverse-engineered Ringg's real-time stack—here's how they handle 20+ accents at sub-337ms latency
best_headline.reason: This headline promises technical depth, appeals directly to the audience’s curiosity about implementation details, and highlights Ringg’s unique claim (latency + accent support) without hype. It positions the piece as a rare, actionable teardown rather than generic funding news.

viral_hooks.social_media_version: Reverse-engineered: How Ringg pulls off sub-337ms voice AI with 20+ accents (and what you can steal from their stack)

hook.opening_line: Imagine calling a factory helpline and, within milliseconds, a hyper-fluent AI answers in your native accent—no waiting, no confusion.
hook.stakes: The race to automate industrial support is heating up, and whoever cracks real-time, multilingual voice AI at scale could redefine how manufacturing firms operate globally.
hook.why_now: Recent leaps in AI and browser telephony make it possible to deliver seamless, human-like voice agents—right as manufacturers face labor shortages and rising customer expectations.

discovery.surface_story: Ringg claims to offer the fastest, most flexible voice AI platform for manufacturing, supporting 20+ languages and instant web call integration.
discovery.deeper_story: Digging deeper, Ringg’s sub-337ms mean latency for 10,000+ concurrent calls isn’t just a spec sheet boast—it signals a custom, highly-optimized inference and telephony stack, with modular knowledge management that lets operators update information on the fly without downtime.
discovery.surprise: Unlike most competitors, Ringg’s no-code platform lets users upload large knowledge bases and decouple them from call flows, drastically reducing operational friction and enabling rapid iteration.

analysis.technical_insight: Ringg’s architecture leverages agentic patterns, retrieval-augmented generation, and vertical data moats to deliver real-time, scalable, and multilingual voice AI—outpacing typical latency benchmarks and simplifying deployment.
analysis.competitive_insight: While Ringg’s tech is impressive, its moat is only medium—giants like OpenAI, Google, or Twilio could theoretically replicate or outscale these capabilities if they prioritize the vertical.
analysis.tension: Bull case: Ringg’s deep manufacturing focus and technical optimizations build a sticky, defensible platform. Bear case: It’s just a slick API wrapper, vulnerable to displacement if hyperscalers enter the space.

resolution.takeaway: Speed, modularity, and deep manufacturing integration set Ringg apart—for now.
resolution.prediction: Expect more verticalized, low-latency voice AI stacks to emerge, with increasing pressure on startups to build unique data moats or specialized workflows.
resolution.call_to_action: Builders: profile your latency, decouple knowledge from flows, and watch for hyperscaler moves. Investors: look for signs of defensibility beyond speed and UI polish.

Flowchart: Knowledge upload and decoupled call logic
Market map: Voice AI in manufacturing vertical

unique_voice_content:
```markdown
## Ringg: Fast, Fluent, ... Forgettable?

Ever tried talking to an AI voice bot and spent half the call waiting for it to catch up? Ringg says they’ve solved that—with voice AI so snappy it replies faster than most humans, even when handling thousands of calls at once. But before you start dreaming of a future where your factory talks back in 20 languages, hold up: speed isn’t the whole story.

### The Core Insight

Ringg’s real claim is ultra-low latency (sub-337ms per call) at industrial scale, with instant voice agent integration for manufacturing. Think of it as Twilio plus ChatGPT, but the “Chat” part answers in real time and can handle your weird regional accent. That’s… actually hard. But here’s the rub: it’s not hard in a way that’s defensible. The technical lift is non-trivial, sure, but it lives in the shadow of giants; if OpenAI, Google, or Twilio decide to throw weight behind voice, the same “magic” becomes a checkbox.

### The Technical Meat

Ringg’s architecture leans on modern patterns: agentic workflows, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), vertical data moats (read: upload your company docs), knowledge graphs, and continuous learning loops. The highlight is latency—they’ve clearly built or tuned their own real-time inference and telephony stack (likely some custom WebRTC/SIP orchestration glued to LLM APIs). Supporting 10,000+ concurrent voice calls, in 20+ languages with accent handling, is no small feat. Most competitors are still stuck in the 400-1000ms range, and that difference *is* perceptible. Their browser integration (direct voice calls from web, not just chat widgets) is slick—this is deep B2B plumbing, not just a fancy front end.

The no-code builder is solid: upload 25MB of docs, build voice flows without touching code, update knowledge bases without breaking call logic. This decoupling is smart, and it’ll save ops folks a lot of headaches. Their analytics go beyond basic call outcomes, tracking “memory recall” and “decision paths”—essentially conversational state explainability, which most voice platforms totally skip. Integration? Ringg supports both Zapier-style no-code and direct SDKs for the usual SaaS suspects (Shopify, HubSpot, Twilio, Zendesk, etc.), plus RESTful APIs. This “come one, come all” strategy is good for adoption.

But here’s what’s missing: proprietary IP. The stack doesn’t show signs of custom LLMs, data network effects, or integrations you couldn’t build in a couple sprints if you had a motivated team and an AWS account. Ringg is, at best, a well-engineered API wrapper around commodity AI and telephony. If the big players get interested, Ringg’s moat evaporates overnight.

### The Honest Assessment

Would I use Ringg? If I’m running a mid-sized manufacturing outfit and need a drop-in, multilingual voice agent tomorrow, maybe. If I’m building for the long haul, and care about defensibility or true conversational intelligence, probably not. The platform is polished and fast, but the underlying ingredients are all shelf-stable and available to anyone with API keys. If you need “fast, generic, and easy,” great. If you want “differentiated and durable,” keep looking.

### The Builder Takeaway

- **Low-latency matters**: If your AI is slow, nobody cares how smart it is.
- **Decouple knowledge from logic**: Modular architectures save your ops team’s sanity.
- **Deep integrations win adoption**: Support both no-code and dev workflows to maximize market.
- **State tracking is under-used**: “Decision path” analytics give you real explainability—steal this idea.
- **Moats require more than dashboards**: If your secret sauce can be API’d away, it’s not much of a sauce.

### The Prediction

In two years, Ringg is either acquired by a bigger SaaS player looking to tick the “voice AI” box, or quietly squeezed out as the cloud giants commoditize the stack. If they demonstrate proprietary models, real conversational breakthroughs, or vertical-specific IP, I’ll gladly eat my words.
```
